p_question-5981.ja:
以下は、留学生の作文である。


日本の飲食店

デュボワガスパール

　私は子どものときから旅行が大好きです。日本でもいろいろなところへ旅行に行きました。日本では、違う町に行っても、ファミレスや定食屋、ファストフード店など、同じような飲食店が多いと感じます。日本各地にはおいしい食べ物がたくさんあるのだから、そこにしかない飲食店に行くほうがいいと思っていました。それで、旅行のときはずっと、その土地にしかない店で食事を [19]。

　旅行先で、行ったことがない店に行くのは楽しいです。[20]、旅行を続けていると、途中で疲れてきます。疲れているとき、慣れない店に行くのは少し大変です。そんなとき、家の近くにもあるファミレスや定食屋が旅行先にもあるのはいいことだと思うようになりました。メニューも注文方法も[21]。私は旅行中に疲れたとき、近所にもあるレストランに入るようになりました。

　どこにでもある同じような店に行くことは、旅行先だけでできる特別な経験ではありませんが、安心感があります。[22] の、今まで知らなかったいいところを見つけることができました。

The following is an essay by an international student.


Restaurants in Japan

Dubois Gaspard

 I have loved traveling since I was a child. I have also traveled to many places in Japan. In Japan, even when you go to different towns, there are many similar kinds of eateries such as family restaurants, set-meal shops, and fast-food places. Because there are lots of delicious foods all over Japan, I thought it would be better to go to restaurants that exist only there. So when I traveled, I always [19] at places that could only be found in that area.

 It’s fun to go to a place I’ve never been to at a travel destination. [20], as you keep traveling, you start getting tired along the way. When you’re tired, going into an unfamiliar place can be a bit hard. At such times, I came to feel it’s nice that the family restaurants and set-meal shops near my house also exist at travel destinations. Both the menu and the way of ordering are [21]. When I get tired while traveling, I started going into restaurants that I also have in my neighborhood.

 Going to the same kinds of places you can find anywhere isn’t a special experience you can only have while traveling, but it gives you a sense of reassurance. Thanks to [22], I was able to discover good points I hadn’t known before.

p_question-5985.ja:
（１）

　なかたしさんの家のテーブルの上に、このメモがある。


　たかしへ

　急な用事で、ちょっとおじいちゃんの所へ行かなければならなくなりました。７時までには帰れると思うけど、おなかがすいて待てなかったら、昨日のカレーの残りを温めて食べてください。

　それから、洗濯物が干したままになっているから、取り込んでおいてね。時間があったら、たたんでおいてください。

　おいしいケーキでも買って帰るね。

母

p_question-5987.ja:
（３）

　駅前の交差点の信号は赤になるのが早く、少し遅れると、途中から走って渡らなければならないことも多かった。しかし、一か月ぐらい前からは走らなくても時間内に渡れるようになった。それで、歩くのが速くなったと思って喜んでいたのだが、今朝の新聞を読んで本当の理由がわかった。あの歩行者用信号は時間内に渡れない人が多かったので、警察が青の時間を少し長くしたのだそうだ。それを知って、ちょっとがっかりしてしまった。

(3)

 The light at the intersection in front of the station used to turn red quickly, and if you were a little late, you often had to run partway to get across. However, from about a month ago, I’ve been able to cross within the time without running. I was pleased, thinking my walking had gotten faster, but after reading this morning’s newspaper I learned the real reason. Many people couldn’t finish crossing within the time at that pedestrian signal, so the police apparently made the green light a little longer. When I learned that, I ended up feeling a bit disappointed.

p_question-5988.ja:
（４）

　これは、ある雑誌の記事である。


　あるアンケートで、朝、歯を磨く人3,000人に、磨くのは「食前」「食後」「食前と食後」のどれかを聞いた。

　性別でみると、男性も女性も「食後」に磨く人が最も多く（男性64%、女性72%）、次は、男性が「食前」で、女性が「食前と食後」だった。

　年齢でみても、各年齢で一番多いのはやはり「食後」だった。しかし、その割合は、年齢が高くなるにしたがって「食後」が減り、「食前」が増えていく。なるほど、うちでは両親は食後に、祖父は食前に磨いてい。

(4)

 This is a magazine article.


 In a certain survey, 3,000 people who brush their teeth in the morning were asked whether they brush “before eating,” “after eating,” or “both before and after eating.”

 By gender, the largest group for both men and women was those who brush “after eating” (men 64%, women 72%). Next, for men it was “before eating,” and for women it was “both before and after eating.”

 Looking by age, in each age group the most common was likewise “after eating.” However, as age increases, the share of “after eating” decreases and “before eating” increases. I see—at my house, my parents brush after meals, and my grandfather brushes before meals.

p_question-5989.ja:
（１）

　最近、近所の花屋が閉店した。30年以上も「町の花屋さん」として愛されてきた店だ。この店がオープンしたのは、わたしがまだ小学校に入る前だった。わたしにとって、①店の思い出はそのまま子どものころの思い出と重なる。家族の誕生日や家にお客さんが来る時などには、母といっしょにこの店で花を買っていた。

　小学校を卒業する時には、こんなことがあった。クラス全員でお金を出し合い、担任の先生に花束をおくることになった。「お礼の気持ちを表すために、見たこともないほど大きいのをおくろう」とわたしたちは話し合った。しかし、小学生のおこづかいの中から集まったお金は少しだけだった。それで、②わたしたちはどきどきしながら、「大好きな先生にあげるから、できるだけ大きい花束を作ってください」とお願いした。おじさんはいやな顔もしないで、特別大きなバラの花束を作ってくれた。

　30年以上もきれいな花束を作り続け、あたたかい思い出を作ってくれたおじさんに、「ありがとう、お疲れ様でした」と言いたい。

(1)

 Recently, the neighborhood flower shop closed down. It was a shop that had been loved as the town’s florist for over 30 years. The shop opened before I had even started elementary school. For me, ① my memories of the shop overlap directly with my childhood memories. For birthdays in the family or when guests were coming to our house, I would go with my mother to buy flowers there.

 When I graduated from elementary school, this happened. Everyone in the class pooled our money to present a bouquet to our homeroom teacher. We discussed, “To show our gratitude, let’s give the biggest one anyone’s ever seen.” However, the money collected from grade-schoolers’ allowances was only a little. So, ② with our hearts pounding, we asked, “It’s for our beloved teacher, so please make the bouquet as large as possible.” The shop owner didn’t look the least bit annoyed and made an especially large bouquet of roses for us.

 To the owner who kept making beautiful bouquets for over 30 years and gave us warm memories, I want to say, “Thank you, and well done for all your hard work.”

p_question-5992.ja:
（２）

　あなたはアイスクリームが好きですか。甘くて冷たいアイスクリームは暑い夏に食べること、体が涼しくなるし、寒い冬に暖かい部屋で食べるのも、おいしいものです。今ではアイスクリームは一年中いつでも食べられますが、①面白いのは、夏と冬ではよく売れるアイスの種類が違うことです。夏にはレモン味のシャーベットなどが、冬にはチョコレート味などの濃い味のものがよく売れるそうです。

　アイスクリームは150年くらい前から食べられるようになったのですが、実は、日本で「氷」を楽しむ習慣は10世紀には始まっていたようです。

　そのころは、冬に作った氷を、「氷室」と呼ばれる特別な部屋に運んで、とけないようにしておきました。夏になると、そこから大きな氷を取り出して町まで運びます。町までは遠く、運ぶ間に少しずつとけて小さくなりますから、②氷はとても大事なものでした。そのころは氷をけずって、さとう水をかけて食べていました。今とは違い、③夏の氷は特別なごちそうだったのでしょう。

(2)

 Do you like ice cream? Sweet, cold ice cream cools your body when you eat it in the hot summer, and it’s also delicious to eat in a warm room in the cold winter. Nowadays you can eat ice cream any time of year, but ① what’s interesting is that the types of ice cream that sell well in summer and winter are different. In summer, things like lemon sherbet sell well, while in winter, rich flavors like chocolate tend to sell well.

 People have been able to eat ice cream for about 150 years, but in fact, it seems the custom of enjoying “ice” in Japan had already begun in the 10th century.

 Back then, ice made in winter was taken to a special room called a “himuro” (ice room) so it wouldn’t melt. When summer came, they would take out large blocks of ice from there and carry them to town. The town was far away, and the ice would gradually melt and get smaller during transport, so ② ice was very precious. At that time, they shaved the ice and poured sugar water over it to eat. Unlike today, ③ summer ice must have been a special treat.

p_question-5995.ja:
10年近く前、①印象的なテレビのコマーシャル（CM）があった。ある洗濯洗剤「X」のCMなのだが、洗ったシーツの香りを女性が楽しんでいるだけなのだ。洗濯洗剤を使う目的は汚れを落とすことなのに、そのCMでは、香りのことばかり言っていて、汚れをよく「落とすことは全く言っていなかった。この洗濯洗剤を作っている会社は、どうしてこんな変なCMにしたのだろうかと思ったが、最近ある本を読んで、その理由がわかった。

　その本には、次のようなことが書かれていた。この会社には昔から汚れをよく落とす洗濯洗剤「A」という人気商品があったが、会社の売り上げ（注）をもっと伸ばすために、新しいタイプの洗濯洗剤を作ることにした。それが「X」だった。「X」は、「A」と値段はあまり変わらないが、汚れを落とすだけでなく、香りも楽しめるのが特長だった。

　ところが、最初、「X」は期待していたようには売れなかった。初めのころに作ったCMでは、消費者に「X」の特長がうまく伝わらなかったのだ。そこで、この会社はそれまでのCMを大きく変えて、「この商品を使えば、いい香りが楽しめて、とてもいい気分で洗濯ができる」というメッセージを強く伝えることにした。そうして新しく作られたのが、②私が見たCMだったのだ。

　そのCMによって、「X」の特長が多くの消費者に伝わったようで、それ以降、「X」もよく売れるようになったそうだ。私がちょっと変だと感じたあのCMは、実は十分に宣伝効果があったのだ。

（注）売り上げをもっと伸ばす：商品を売って会社や店などに入るお金をもっと増やす。

Nearly 10 years ago, there was an ① unforgettable TV commercial (CM). It was a commercial for a laundry detergent “X,” but all it showed was a woman enjoying the scent of freshly washed sheets. Even though the purpose of using laundry detergent is to remove dirt, that commercial talked only about the fragrance and didn’t say anything at all about how well it “removes” dirt. I wondered why the company that made this detergent would create such a strange commercial, but after reading a certain book recently, I understood the reason.

 The book said the following. This company had long had a popular product, a laundry detergent “A” that removes dirt well, but in order to further increase the company’s sales (note), they decided to create a new type of laundry detergent. That was “X.” Although the price of “X” wasn’t much different from “A,” its feature was that in addition to removing dirt, you could also enjoy the fragrance.

 However, at first “X” didn’t sell as well as expected. In the commercials made in the early days, the features of “X” weren’t effectively communicated to consumers. Therefore, the company drastically changed the commercials and decided to strongly convey the message that “if you use this product, you can enjoy a nice fragrance and feel great while doing laundry.” The commercial newly created that way was the ② CM I saw.

 Thanks to that commercial, it seems the features of “X” were conveyed to many consumers, and after that, “X” reportedly began to sell well, too. That commercial I felt was a bit strange actually had plenty of advertising effect.

(Note) “Increase sales further”: to increase the money that comes into a company or store from selling products.

questions.stem: このビルの裏に小さなレストランがあります。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 4: うら is correct.

**Question Translation**  
"このビルの裏に小さなレストランがあります。"  
→ "There is a small restaurant behind this building."

**Explanation**  
The kanji "裏" is read as **うら**, which means *behind* or *back*. This reading is appropriate in the context of the sentence, talking about something located behind the building.

**Other Options**  
1. そば – Means *near* or *beside* (kanji: 側), not appropriate for 裏.  
2. よこ – Means *side* or *beside* (kanji: 横), does not match 裏.  
3. かげ – Means *shadow* or *shade* (kanji: 影), which is unrelated to 裏.

Only **うら** fits the reading and meaning of the kanji **裏** in this context.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「裏(うら)」 — 뒤, 뒷쪽.
오답: 1 そば(곁·옆=側), 2 よこ(옆=横), 3 かげ(그늘=陰)는 의미는 비슷해도 「裏」의 음·훈은 아님.
포인트: 「ビルの裏」=빌딩 뒤편. 裏=うら(훈독).

questions.stem: それではゆっくり呼吸をしてください。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 2: こきゅう is correct.

**Question Translation**  
"それではゆっくり呼吸をしてください。"  
→ "Then, please breathe slowly."

**Explanation**  
The kanji "呼吸" is read as **こきゅう**, which means *breathing*. This is the correct reading based on the kanji in the sentence, as it makes sense contextually with the instruction to breathe slowly.

**Other Options**  
1. こぎゅう – Not a standard reading of 呼吸.  
3. よぎゅう – Not a standard reading and does not fit the kanji 呼吸.  
4. よきゅう – Suggests *demand* or *request* (kanji: 要求), which does not match 呼吸.

Only **こきゅう** matches the kanji **呼吸** in this context.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「呼吸(こきゅう)」 — 호흡.
오답: 1 こぎゅう·3 よぎゅう·4 よきゅう는 모두 존재하지 않는 음. 呼=コ, 吸=キュウ.
포인트: 「ゆっくり呼吸する」=천천히 호흡하다. 탁음 ぎゅう가 아니라 청음 きゅう.

questions.stem: あの少年の悲しそうな顔が忘れられない。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 2: かなしそう is correct.

**Question Translation**  
"あの少年の悲しそうな顔が忘れられない。"  
→ "I can't forget that boy's sad-looking face."

**Explanation**  
The kanji "悲しそう" is read as **かなしそう**, which means *sad-looking*. This is the appropriate reading for the kanji used in the sentence, which describes a boy's face as appearing sad.

**Other Options**  
1. やさしそう – Means *kind-looking* (kanji: 優しそう), not appropriate here.  
3. きびしそう – Means *strict-looking* (kanji: 厳しそう), and doesn't fit in this context.  
4. さびしそう – Means *lonely-looking* (kanji: 寂しそう), and is not the correct match for 悲しそう.  

Only **かなしそう** is the correct reading for **悲しそう** in this sentence.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「悲(かな)しそう」 — 슬퍼 보이는.
오답: 1 やさしそう(優しそう=다정해 보인다), 3 きびしそう(厳しそう=엄해 보인다), 4 さびしそう(寂しそう=쓸쓸해 보인다)는 모두 다른 형용사.
포인트: 悲=かな(しい). 「〜そう」(=様態)와 결합.

questions.stem: このあたりに駐車しましょう。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 4: ちゅうしゃ is correct.

**Question Translation**  
"このあたりに駐車しましょう。"  
→ "Let's park around here."

**Explanation**  
The kanji "駐車" is read as **ちゅうしゃ**, which means *parking*. This is the correct reading based on the kanji in the sentence that deals with parking a vehicle.

**Other Options**  
1. しゅしゃ – Not a standard reading of 駐車.  
2. しゅうしゃ – Incorrect reading for 駐車, does not convey the meaning of *parking*.  
3. ちゅしゃ – Misses a sound, not matching the correct reading for 駐車.

Only **ちゅうしゃ** correctly matches the kanji **駐車** for this context.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「駐車(ちゅうしゃ)」 — 주차.
오답: 1 しゅしゃ·2 しゅうしゃ·3 ちゅしゃ는 없는 음. 駐=チュウ(장음).
포인트: 장음 「ちゅう」 주의. 「駐車場(ちゅうしゃじょう)」도 동일.

questions.stem: もう逃げてしまったようだ。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 1: にげて is correct.

**Question Translation**  
"もう逃げてしまったようだ。"  
→ "It seems they have already escaped."

**Explanation**  
The kanji "逃げて" is read as **にげて**, which means *to escape* or *to run away*. This reading matches the context of the sentence, which suggests someone has already fled.

**Other Options**  
2. なげて – Means *to throw* (kanji: 投げて), and does not match 逃げて.  
3. こげて – Means *burnt* or *scorched* (kanji: 焦げて), and is not suitable here.  
4. あげて – Means *to raise* or *to give (a gift)* (kanji: 上げて/挙げて), unrelated to 逃げて.

Only **にげて** corresponds to the kanji **逃げて** in this context.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「逃(に)げて」 — 도망쳐서.
오답: 2 なげて(投げて=던지다), 3 こげて(焦げて=타다), 4 あげて(上げて=올리다)는 모두 다른 한자.
포인트: 逃=に(げる). 「〜てしまった」(=완료·유감)와 결합.

questions.stem: 努力することは大切だと思います。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 3: どりょく is correct.

**Question Translation**  
"努力することは大切だと思います。"  
→ "I think making an effort is important."

**Explanation**  
The kanji "努力" is read as **どりょく**, which means *effort* or *endeavor*. This is the correct reading based on the kanji in the sentence, emphasizing the importance of putting in effort.

**Other Options**  
1. どうりょく – Not a standard reading of 努力.  
2. とうりょく – Not a recognized reading for this word.  
4. とりょく – Incorrect reading and does not match 努力.

Only **どりょく** matches the kanji **努力** in this context.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「努力(どりょく)」 — 노력.
오답: 1 どうりょく·2 とうりょく(→動力의 음과 헷갈림), 4 とりょく는 없는 음. 努=ド(단음), 力=リョク.
포인트: 「努」는 장음이 아닌 단음 ど. 「努力する」(노력하다).

questions.stem: それは過去のことです。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 1: かこ is correct.

**Question Translation**  
"それは過去のことです。"  
→ "That is a thing of the past."

**Explanation**  
The kanji "過去" is read as **かこ**, which means *the past*. This is the correct reading based on the kanji in the sentence context, indicating something that has already happened.

**Other Options**  
2. かこう – Refers to a possibility/volitional form (kanji: 可能) but not appropriate here.  
3. かきよ – Not a standard reading for any meaningful kanji phrase here.  
4. かきょう – Could refer to *scam* or *cheat* (kanji: 詐欺), but this is not related to 過去.

Only **かこ** matches the kanji **過去** in this sentence.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「過去(かこ)」 — 과거.
오답: 2 かこう(加工·下降 등 다른 어휘의 음), 3 かきよ·4 かきょう는 없는 음.

questions.stem: 彼は話し方も動作もゆっくりしている。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 3: どうさ is correct.

**Question Translation**  
"彼は話し方も動作もゆっくりしている。"  
→ "He speaks and moves slowly."

**Explanation**  
The kanji "動作" is read as **どうさ**, which means *movement* or *action*. This is the correct reading based on the kanji in the sentence.

**Other Options**  
1. とうさ – Not a standard reading of 動作.  
2. とうさく – Not applicable to 動作, may cause confusion with 作 (create or make).  
4. どうさく – Suggests some wordplay with 動 (move) and 作 (create), but not a reading for 動作.

Only **どうさ** matches the kanji **動作** for the context of movements or actions.
questions.expl_ko:

questions.stem: 昨日、友達が家にとまりにきた。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 3: 泊まり is correct.

**Question Translation**  
"昨日、友達が家にとまりにきた。"  
→ "Yesterday, my friend came over to stay (the night) at my house."

**Explanation**  
The word "とまり" in this context means *to stay overnight*, and the correct kanji representation is **泊まり**. This kanji is used when referring to staying over at someone's place or lodging at a hotel.

**Other Options**  
1. 停まり（とまり）– This reading is the same, "とまり", and refers to *stopping*, as in stopping a vehicle or a scheduled stop. It does not fit the meaning of staying overnight.

2. 留まり（とどまり）– Read as "とどまり", which means *to remain* or *stay in place* in a non-residential context. It’s not applicable for a sleepover or lodging.

4. 止まり（とまり）– Similar to "停まり", also read as "とまり", and means *to stop*. This is used in terms of movement and is not suitable here.

Only **泊まり** accurately conveys the intended meaning of "to stay overnight" in the context of the sentence.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「泊まり」 — 묵음, 숙박.
오답: 1 停(と)まる(차가 멈추다), 2 留(と)まる(고정·머무름), 4 止(と)まる(멈추다)는 모두 「とまる」지만 의미가 다름.
포인트: 「家にとまる」=집에 묵다 → 숙박은 「泊」. 「お泊まり」.

questions.stem: 書類にはこの学校のきそくが書かれていた。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 2: 規則 is correct.

**Question Translation**  
"書類にはこの学校のきそくが書かれていた。"  
→ "The document had the rules of this school written on it."

**Explanation**  
The word "きそく" means *rules* or *regulations*, and the correct kanji representation is **規則**. This term is often used for rules or guidelines, especially in formal settings like schools or workplaces.

**Other Options**  
1. 現販（げんはん）– The reading of this kanji is "げんはん", and it means *current sales*. It does not relate to the meaning of "きそく".

3. 規販（きはん）– The reading is "きはん", but this is not a standard kanji combination and does not convey any recognized meaning, thus not suitable here.

4. 現則（げんそく）– This is read as "げんそく", meaning *current principles*, though this is not a common or standard expression for "きそく".

Only **規則** correctly represents the word "きそく" in this sentence.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「規則(きそく)」 — 규칙.
오답: 1 現販·3 規販·4 現則은 모두 존재하지 않는 단어. 「販」(팔다)은 「則」과 형태가 비슷한 함정.
포인트: 規=キ(법칙·규범), 則=ソク(법칙). 「学校のきそく」=학교 규칙.

questions.stem: ここはあたたかいですね。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 3: 暖かい is correct.

**Question Translation**  
"ここはあたたかいですね。"  
→ "It's warm here, isn't it?"

**Explanation**  
The word "あたたかい" means *warm*, particularly in the context of air temperature or the warmth of a person or setting. The correct kanji representation is **暖かい**. It is typically used to describe a comfortable and pleasant warmth.

**Other Options**  
1. 明るい（あかるい）– The reading of this kanji is "あかるい", meaning *bright* or *cheerful*. It doesn’t match the meaning of "あたたかい".

2. 暑かい（あつかい）– The correct reading for "暑" is "あつい", and it means *hot* (used for weather). This kanji does not match "あたたかい".

4. 熱い（あつい）– The reading is "あつい", which also means *hot* but typically refers to objects or sensations. It is not used for the warm meaning of "あたたかい".

Only **暖かい** correctly fits the word "あたたかい" in this context.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「暖かい」 — (공기·기온이) 따뜻하다.
오답: 1 明(あか)るい(밝다)는 음이 다름. 2 暑(あつ)い는 「あつい」(덥다)로 읽음. 4 熱(あつ)い(뜨겁다)도 「あつい」.
포인트: 기온의 따뜻함=「暖かい」, 액체·물건의 따뜻함=「温かい」.

questions.stem: やっきょくがなかなか見つかりません。

This kanji is specific when referring to a pharmacy or a drugstore. **Other Options** 2. 楽曲（がっきょく）– The reading of this kanji is "がっきょく", and it means *musical piece*. It does not match the word "やっきょく". 3. 楽局（がくきょく）– The reading for this mistakenly written kanji could be "がくきょく", which isn’t a recognized word and does not match "やっきょく". 4. Repeat of Answer 2, 楽曲（がっきょく）– Again, this means *musical piece* and is not suitable for "やっきょく". Only **薬局** accurately represents what is needed in the context of the sentence which involves finding a pharmacy.

questions.stem: お皿はかさねてその棚にしまってください。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 4: 重ねて is correct.

**Question Translation**  
"お皿はかさねてその棚にしまってください。"  
→ "Please stack the plates and put them in that shelf."

**Explanation**  
The word "かさねて" means *to stack* or *to pile*, and the correct kanji representation is **重ねて**. This verb form is commonly used with objects like plates or papers that are placed on top of each other.

**Other Options**  
1. 整れて（ととのれて）– This reading is "ととのれて", which means *to be arranged* or *to be organized*. It doesn’t convey the idea of stacking.

2. 列れて（れつれて）– The reading is "れつれて", which implies lining up, often unclear in meaning and not commonly used.

3. 階れて（かいれて）– This is read as "かいれて" and is not a valid usage in this context, as it does not make sense in relation to stacking.

Only **重ねて** appropriately represents the word "かさねて" in this sentence about stacking plates.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「重ねて」 — 겹쳐서, 포개서.
오답: 1 整れて·2 列れて·3 階れて는 존재하지 않는 동사.
포인트: 「お皿をかさねる」=접시를 포개다. 重=かさ(ねる).

questions.stem: 机の上にでんごんのメモがあった。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 2: 伝言 is correct. It means “(a) message” (something passed on to someone). Reading: でんごん.

**Question Translation**  
There was a message note (a message memo) on the desk.

**Explanation**  
The sentence says でんごんのメモ, which commonly refers to a 伝言メモ (message memo), like a note left with a message (often for phone calls). 伝言 (でんごん) precisely means “message to be conveyed,” fitting both the reading and the context.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: 伝記 (でんき) means “biography.” The reading and meaning don’t match でんごん or the context.  
- Answer 3: 転記 (てんき) means “transcription/copying entries” (e.g., in ledgers). Wrong reading and unnatural in this context.  
- Answer 4: 転勤 (てんきん) means “job transfer.” Wrong reading and doesn’t fit “...のメモ” here.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
- 伝言メモ is a common set phrase for a written message note.  
- の links two nouns: 伝言 (message) + メモ (memo) → “a memo of a message.”
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「伝言(でんごん)」 — 전언, 메시지.
오답: 1 伝記(でんき=전기), 3 転記(てんき=옮겨 적기), 4 転勤(てんきん=전근)은 의미·음 모두 다름.
포인트: 「でんごんのメモ」=전언 메모. 伝=デン, 言=ゴン(특수음).

questions.stem: マラソン大会は台風で（　　　）されることになりました。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 3: 延期 (えんき) is correct. It means "postponement."

**Question Translation**  
The marathon event has been decided to be postponed due to the typhoon.

**Explanation**  
延期 (えんき) means "to postpone" or "to delay." In the context of a marathon event being affected by a typhoon, the most logical action is to postpone the event to a later date for safety reasons. The use of 延期 fits the context of the sentence, which is about making a decision to change the timing of the event due to unforeseen circumstances.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: 遅刻 (ちこく) means "lateness" or "to be late." This term is typically used for individuals being late, not for rescheduling events.
- Answer 2: 連休 (れんきゅう) means "consecutive holidays" or "long weekend." It doesn’t apply here because it neither relates to an event being rescheduled nor reflects any logical action related to a typhoon.
- Answer 4: 早退 (そうたい) means "leaving early" or "early departure." It doesn't fit the context of an event being impacted by weather, as it doesn’t describe a change to the event’s schedule.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
延期 (えんき) is a noun that can be used with the verb される (passive form) to indicate that an event is decided or arranged to be postponed. This is a common expression used in contexts where events need to be rescheduled due to various circumstances like weather, venue availability, etc. Recognizing contextually-appropriate vocabulary is crucial for understanding nuanced Japanese expressions.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「延期(えんき)」 — 연기.
오답: 1 遅刻(ちこく=지각), 2 連休(れんきゅう=연휴), 4 早退(そうたい=조퇴)는 행사를 미루는 의미가 아님.
포인트: 「台風で〜される」=태풍으로 ~되다 → 행사가 뒤로 미뤄지는 것은 「延期」.

questions.stem: 毎日水をやらないと、花が（　　　）しまう。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 4: かれて (枯れて) is correct. It means "to wither" or "to dry up," referring to plants.

**Question Translation**  
If you don't water them every day, the flowers will wither.

**Explanation**  
The context of the sentence is about the consequence of not watering flowers daily. In this case, "かれて" (枯れて) is the most appropriate choice because flowers wilting or drying up due to lack of water perfectly fits the scenario described. This verb is often used to describe what happens to plants when they don't receive enough water.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: とけて (溶けて) means "to melt" or "to dissolve." This is used for situations involving substances melting or dissolving, not plants.
- Answer 2: さめて (冷めて) means "to get cold" or "to cool down." This term is typically used for temperature changes in liquids or food, not for describing the state of plants.
- Answer 3: やせて (痩せて) means "to become thin." This is primarily used for living beings, indicating a decrease in body mass, not for withering plants.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The term "枯れる" (かれる) is a specific vocabulary word often used to describe plants losing their vitality due to insufficient water, change in seasons, or poor soil conditions. Recognizing this word in context helps identify that it refers to plants and is related to care practices like watering.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「かれて(枯れて)」 — (꽃·식물이) 시들다.
오답: 1 とけて(녹다), 2 さめて(식다), 3 やせて(마르다·살이 빠지다)는 꽃이 죽는 상황과 맞지 않음.
포인트: 식물에 물을 안 주면 → 「枯れる」(시들다).

questions.stem: この犬は、子犬のころから（　　　）されているので、人が大勢いてもおとなしい。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 3: 訓練 (くんれん) is correct. It means "training."

**Question Translation**  
This dog is trained from when it was a puppy, so it remains calm even when there are many people around.

**Explanation**  
The sentence describes how the dog behaves calmly even when surrounded by many people because of something done since it was a puppy. The word 訓練 (training) indicates that the dog was trained to behave a certain way, which fits the context as it refers to a process of teaching the dog desired behaviors or commands.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: 工夫 (くふう) means "ingenuity" or "devise." It is used for creative solutions or contrivances and does not fit the context of consistently shaping or conditioning behavior like training does.  
- Answer 2: 用意 (ようい) means "preparation" or "arrangement." This does not involve the active process of instilling behaviors or skills, which is necessary for explaining the dog’s behavior.  
- Answer 4: 計画 (けいかく) means "plan." It refers to forming a plan or strategy, not the process of conditioning or teaching, which is necessary to influence a dog’s behavior from puppyhood.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The kanji 訓練 is specifically used to describe disciplined learning or the acquisition of skills or habits. Understanding how specific words like 訓練 apply to context helps determine the appropriateness in sentences involving behavioral conditioning or skills.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「訓練(くんれん)」 — 훈련.
오답: 1 工夫(くふう=궁리), 2 用意(ようい=준비), 4 計画(けいかく=계획)는 「개가 사람에 익숙해진 이유」와 맞지 않음.
포인트: 「子犬のころから〜されている」=강아지 때부터 ~되어 있다 → 「訓練」.

questions.stem: 明日は７時に出かけるので、目覚まし時計を６時に（　　　）した。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 2: セット is correct. It means "set."

**Question Translation**  
Tomorrow, I will leave at 7 o'clock, so I set my alarm clock for 6 o'clock.

**Explanation**  
In the context of setting an alarm clock, the verb セットする (setto suru) is commonly used to mean "to set" (as in setting a time for the alarm to go off). The sentence is discussing preparations for an event happening the next day, which involves adjusting the alarm clock to ensure an early departure. Therefore, セット is the appropriate choice as it describes the action of adjusting or setting the alarm clock to ring at a particular time. 

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: スタート (start) means "to start." It doesn't fit in this context because you don't "start" an alarm for a specific time; rather, you "set" it.
- Answer 3: ストップ (stop) means "to stop." This is not applicable in the sentence because stopping an alarm would imply turning it off rather than preparing it to ring.
- Answer 4: マーク (mark) means "to mark." Marking doesn't convey the intended action of adjusting an alarm clock to ring at a specific time.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The verb セットする is a loan word from English and is frequently used in Japanese when talking about configuring or adjusting settings, especially with electronic devices like clocks and timers. It’s important to recognize this borrowed term as part of everyday Japanese usage in various contexts beyond just alarm clocks.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「セット」 — 세트, 맞춤.
오답: 1 スタート(시작), 3 ストップ(정지), 4 マーク(표시)는 알람을 '설정한다'는 의미와 어긋남.
포인트: 「目覚まし時計をセットする」=알람시계를 (시간에) 맞추다.

questions.stem: 忙しいのに、こんなに待たされて時間が（　　　）。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 4: もったいない is correct. It means "wasteful" or "a waste," often used when something valuable is not being utilized adequately.

**Question Translation**  
Despite being busy, having to wait like this is a waste of my time.

**Explanation**  
In the context of this sentence, the speaker is expressing frustration about time being spent inefficiently when they are already busy. The word もったいない (mottainai) aptly describes the feeling of time being wasted, as it reflects a sense of regret about not making the best use of a valuable resource—in this case, time.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: だらしない (darashinai) means "sloppy" or "disorganized." This doesn’t fit the context because the sentence is about the wastefulness of time, not someone's demeanor or behavior.
- Answer 2: しょうがない (shouganai) means "can't be helped" or "inevitable." Although relevant to situations where something can't be avoided, it doesn't align with the expression of wastefulness or regret in this case.
- Answer 3: なさけない (nasakenai) means "pitiful" or "shameful." This doesn’t fit well in the context; the speaker is not necessarily expressing pity or shame, but rather regret over wasted time.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The phrase "もったいない" is often used to express the waste of anything perceived as valuable, such as time, resources, or opportunities. It embodies a cultural emphasis on appreciating and efficiently using available resources, a concept deeply ingrained in Japanese society. Understanding the usage of this expression can help in identifying nuanced sentiments in similar contexts.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「もったいない」 — 아깝다.
오답: 1 だらしない(칠칠치 못하다), 2 しょうがない(어쩔 수 없다), 3 なさけない(한심하다)는 시간의 '아까움'을 직접 나타내지 않음.
포인트: 「時間がもったいない」=시간이 아깝다. 바쁜데 기다리는 시간이 아까움.

questions.stem: 友人の家で食べたケーキがおいしかったので、（　　　）と作り方を教えてもらった。

Answer 2: 材料 is correct. It means "ingredients" or "materials."

**Question Translation**  
The cake I ate at my friend's house was so delicious that I asked to be taught the recipe and the ingredients.

**Explanation**  
The context of the sentence is about making a cake, where "ingredients" is the most appropriate word for what one would ask about when wanting to recreate a dish or follow a recipe. 材料 (ざいりょう) specifically refers to the items or substances used in cooking or making something, which perfectly fits this context.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: 資源 (しげん) means "resources" and is generally used in larger contexts like natural resources. It is not typically used for culinary ingredients.
- Answer 3: 部品 (ぶひん) means "parts" or "components" and is often used in mechanical or electronic contexts, such as parts of a machine or electronics.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
Understanding the use of 材料 (ざいりょう) is essential in contexts related to cooking, construction, or even crafting, where the focus is on what something is made with. It’s a common term used across various fields, particularly in recipes.

오답: 1 資源(자원), 3 部品(부품), 4 原因(원인)은 케이크 만드는 재료를 가리키지 않음.

questions.stem: このソファーの愛用（　　　）にアンケートをした。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 3: 者 (しゃ) is correct. It means "person" or "user."

**Question Translation**  
We conducted a survey of the users of this sofa.

**Explanation**  
In this context, 愛用者 (あいようしゃ) means "users" or "enthusiasts." The phrase 愛用 (あいよう) indicates things that people use regularly because they are favorites, and when combined with 者 (しゃ), it refers to the individuals who use or favor the item — in this case, the sofa.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: 生 (せい) means "student" but doesn’t pair with 愛用 to form a meaningful or correct term in this context.  
- Answer 2: 人 (ひと) means "person" but 爱用人 is not a standard compound noun in Japanese. It cannot create a recognized phrase for "users" here.  
- Answer 4: 員 (いん) means "member," often used in compounds that refer to people within specific roles, like 会社員 (かいしゃいん, company employee), but doesn't fit with 愛用 to indicate usage. 

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
Knowing common compound words is essential in Japanese. 者 (しゃ) is frequently used in constructs where it denotes someone who performs the action of the preceding term. Here, 愛用者 elegantly conveys "users" of a particular product, an expression used in contexts like surveys or customer feedback.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「者(しゃ)」 — ~자, ~사람(접미어).
오답: 1 生(せい)·2 人·4 員은 「愛用~」와 결합해 일반 사용자를 가리키지 않음.
포인트: 「愛用者(あいようしゃ)」=애용자. 「N+者」=그 일을 하는 사람.

questions.stem: 工事のせいで道路が（　　　）していて、車がなかなか前に進まない。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 1: 渋滞 (じゅうたい) is correct. It means "traffic jam" or "congestion."

**Question Translation**  
Because of the construction work, the road is congested, and cars cannot move forward easily.

**Explanation**  
In the context of road conditions being affected by construction, 渋滞 is the most suitable choice. This term is specifically used to describe situations where traffic is slow or stopped due to congestion, which aligns with the description that cars cannot move forward easily.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 2: 故障 (こうしょう) means "breakdown" or "malfunction." This term typically refers to mechanical failures, not traffic congestion.
- Answer 3: 増加 (ぞうか) means "increase." While it might describe the increase in traffic, it does not convey the idea of congestion or blocking which is needed to describe why cars can't move.
- Answer 4: 集合 (しゅうごう) means "gathering" or "assembly." This term relates to people or items collecting or assembling, not the clogging or blocking of traffic flow.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The understanding of contextually-defined expressions is crucial. In this sentence, recognizing the typical association of terms with traffic situations helps in selecting 渋滞. Compound words like 渋滞 often appear in descriptions of urban or road conditions and are important to know for understanding transportation and city planning contexts.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「渋滞(じゅうたい)」 — 정체.
오답: 2 故障(고장), 3 増加(증가), 4 集合(집합)은 '도로가 막혀 차가 못 가는' 상황과 맞지 않음.
포인트: 「工事のせいで道路が渋滞する」=공사로 도로가 정체되다.

questions.stem: くすりの（　　　）が出て、少し具合がよくなりました。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 2: 効果 is correct. It means "effect" or "efficacy."

**Question Translation**  
The effectiveness of the medicine showed, and I felt a bit better.

**Explanation**  
This sentence is describing the outcome of taking medicine, which in this context refers to how well it worked. The word 効果 (こうか) means "effect" or "efficacy," which fits perfectly here as it implies that the medicine had a positive impact, improving the person's condition.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: 成績 (せいせき) means "results" or "grades." Although it's about results, it’s typically used in educational settings to refer to grades, not the effectiveness of a medicine.
- Answer 3: 応援 (おうえん) means "support" or "cheering." This doesn’t fit the context of medicine; it’s more suited to contexts like sports or moral support.
- Answer 4: 価値 (かち) means "value" or "worth." While medicine can have value, this term doesn’t relate to the immediate effect or results from taking a medicine.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
効果 (こうか) is a common term used to discuss the results or impact of actions, objects, or medications. Recognizing context is key when dealing with words that describe outcomes or results, as each term can have specific nuances depending on the field (education, support, economy, etc.).
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「効果(こうか)」 — 효과.
오답: 1 成績(성적), 3 応援(응원), 4 価値(가치)는 약의 '효능'을 가리키지 않음.
포인트: 「くすりの効果が出る」=약의 효과가 나타나다.

questions.stem: 彼は日本にまだ一年しか住んでいないのに、日本語が（　　　）だ。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 4: ペラペラ is correct. It means "fluent" when referring to language ability.

**Question Translation**  
He has lived in Japan for only one year, yet his Japanese is fluent.

**Explanation**  
The context of the sentence implies that the speaker is surprised by someone's proficiency in Japanese despite a short duration of residence in Japan. The word ペラペラ (pera pera) is used to describe someone who can speak a language fluently and effortlessly, which fits perfectly here given the context.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: さらさら (sara sara) means "smooth" or "silky," often used to describe hair or water. It doesn’t describe language fluency.
- Answer 2: ばらばら (bara bara) means "scattered" or "disjointed," typically implying disorder. It does not convey the idea of fluent language skills.
- Answer 3: ふらふら (fura fura) means "unsteady" or "dizzy," often used to describe someone who is about to faint or is physically unstable. It is inappropriate for describing language ability.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
ペラペラ is an onomatopoeic term that captures the sound of smooth and continuous speech, much like how a fluent speaker would sound. Recognizing such onomatopoeic expressions is important in Japanese, as they often capture the nuance of actions and states vividly.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「ペラペラ」 — (외국어가) 유창하게.
오답: 1 さらさら(보송보송·졸졸), 2 ばらばら(흩어진 모양), 3 ふらふら(휘청)는 언어 능력과 무관.
포인트: 「日本語がペラペラだ」=일본어가 유창하다. 의태어 「ペラペラ」.

questions.stem: この学校で一生懸命に勉強して、知識や技術を（　　　）と思っている。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 1: 身につけたい is correct. It means "want to acquire" or "want to master."

**Question Translation**  
At this school, I want to study hard to acquire knowledge and skills.

**Explanation**  
The phrase 身につける (みにつける) means "to acquire" or "to master," typically referring to gaining knowledge or skills. In the context of the sentence, the speaker expresses a desire to study diligently to gain knowledge and skills, making 身につけたい (to want to acquire) the most appropriate choice.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 2: 気に入りたい (きにいりたい) means "want to be liked" or "want to be pleased with." This does not fit the context as it relates more to preference or approval rather than the acquisition of knowledge or skills.
- Answer 3: 押し込みたい (おしこみたい) means "want to push in" or "want to stuff in." This is unrelated to the context of learning or acquiring skills.
- Answer 4: 取りあげたい (とりあげたい) means "want to take up" or "want to adopt." This implies selecting or adopting something, which does not match the context of obtaining knowledge or skills.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The expression "身につける" is commonly used to describe the process of learning and mastering skills or knowledge. Understanding this phrasal verb is crucial for interpreting contexts related to education and personal development in Japanese.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「身につけたい」 — 익히고 싶다, 자기 것으로 만들고 싶다.
오답: 2 気に入りたい(?·비문), 3 押し込みたい(밀어 넣다), 4 取りあげたい(집어 들다·빼앗다)는 지식·기술 습득 의미가 아님.
포인트: 「知識・技術を身につける」=지식·기술을 익히다(체득하다).

questions.stem: 今日の会議ではさまざまな意見が出た。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 3: いろいろな is correct. It means "various" or "a variety of."

**Question Translation**  
In today's meeting, various opinions were presented.

**Explanation**  
The phrase さまざま (様々) means "various" and is often used to indicate a range of differing items, ideas, or opinions. In the context of a meeting where different opinions were shared, いろいろな (いろいろな) is an appropriate paraphrase because it similarly means "various" or "a variety of." Both convey the idea of diversity and variation in the opinions that were introduced.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: とくべつな (特別な) means "special" or "particular." It doesn't fit because the sentence is about the variety, not the distinctiveness, of opinions.
- Answer 2: すばらしい (素晴らしい) means "wonderful" or "excellent." This does not match the intended meaning of expressing diversity in opinions.
- Answer 4: あたらしい (新しい) means "new." The context does not call for new ideas, but rather a range of different opinions.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
さまざま (様々) is an adjective commonly used to express the concept of diversity in elements such as ideas, colors, or items. Understanding similar adjectives like いろいろ (色々) that convey a similar idea of variety is key in paraphrasing questions on the JLPT. Recognizing these synonyms helps in understanding context-driven questions.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「いろいろな」 — 여러 가지의.
오답: 1 とくべつな(특별한), 2 すばらしい(훌륭한), 4 あたらしい(새로운)는 '다양한'의 뜻이 아님.
포인트: 「さまざま(様々)」≒「いろいろ」 — 다양함을 나타내는 동의어.

questions.stem: できるだけ早めに報告してください。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 2: 知らせて is correct. It means "notify" or "let know."

**Question Translation**  
Please notify me as soon as possible.

**Explanation**  
The sentence requests action to be taken promptly, specifically to "report" or "inform" someone. The word "報告して" (ほうこくして), derived from "報告" (ほうこく, meaning "report" or "inform"), corresponds with "知らせて" (しらせて), which means to inform or notify. This aligns with the sentence asking someone to convey information quickly.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: 頼んで (たのんで) means "to ask" or "request." It doesn't match "report," as the focus is on soliciting rather than informing.
- Answer 3: 尋ねて (たずねて) means "to inquire" or "ask." This involves seeking information, which is opposite to providing it as "報告" would necessitate.
- Answer 4: 探して (さがして) means "to search" or "look for." This indicates an action to find something, unrelated to notifying or reporting information.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The phrase "知らせて" is often used when asking someone to inform or update you about something, especially in contexts requiring communication about changes or developments. Recognizing the context in which these verbs are used can help in selecting the appropriate option in paraphrase or synonym questions.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「知らせて」 — 알려.
오답: 1 頼んで(부탁하다), 3 尋ねて(묻다), 4 探して(찾다)는 '보고하다'의 뜻이 아님.
포인트: 「報告する」≒「知らせる」 — 알리다.

questions.stem: 山田さんは絶対に来ると思います。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 4: かならず is correct. It means "certainly" or "without fail."

**Question Translation**  
I think Yamada-san will certainly come.

**Explanation**  
The word 絶対に (ぜったいに) in Japanese means "absolutely" or "without fail." It is used to express a high degree of certainty about something happening. The word かならず (必ず) is a perfect synonym in this context, meaning "certainly" or "without fail," thus it matches the meaning and nuance of 絶対に in the sentence.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: あとで means "later." It refers to a time-related aspect but does not convey certainty.
- Answer 2: すぐに means "immediately." While it relates to timing, it doesn't convey the certainty aspect.
- Answer 3: たぶん means "probably" or "maybe." It indicates uncertainty, which contrasts with 絶対に.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
Understanding the nuance and context of words expressing certainty or probability is crucial. 絶対に and かならず are both used to convey strong certainty, whereas words like たぶん imply uncertainty or probability, which changes the intended meaning significantly. Being able to recognize these subtle differences is key in paraphrase questions.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「かならず(必ず)」 — 반드시.
오답: 1 あとで(나중에), 2 すぐに(곧), 3 たぶん(아마도)은 확신의 강도·시점이 다름. 특히 3 「たぶん」은 약한 추측으로 「絶対に」와 어긋남.
포인트: 「絶対に〜」=절대로(반드시) ~. 「かならず〜」=꼭 ~.

questions.stem: この話はおしまいです。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 1: おわり is correct. It means "end."

**Question Translation**  
This story is at its end.

**Explanation**  
The sentence declares the conclusion of a story. The word おしまい (oshimai) is a synonym for おわり (owari), which also means "end" or "conclusion." They are used interchangeably in contexts where something is finishing or coming to a close.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 2: すごい (sugoi) means "amazing" or "incredible." This word describes something noteworthy or exciting but does not convey any sense of ending, which is needed in the sentence.
- Answer 3: はじめて (hajimete) means "for the first time" or "beginning." It is the opposite in meaning to "end," making it inappropriate for this context.
- Answer 4: おもしろい (omoshiroi) means "interesting" or "funny." While it can describe the quality of the story, it does not indicate the story's conclusion.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The term おしまい (oshimai) is often used informally to indicate the end of something, such as a story, a presentation, or a performance. It's important to recognize synonyms in Japanese to understand context better, as they can often be substituted for one another depending on the speaker or text's style.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「おわり」 — 끝.
오답: 2 すごい(대단하다), 3 はじめて(처음으로), 4 おもしろい(재미있다)는 '끝'과 무관.
포인트: 「おしまい」≒「おわり」 — 구어적 '끝'.

questions.stem: 先週、学校をサボってしまった。
opts: 病気になって学校をやめてしまった。
病気になって学校を休んでしまった。
あそびたくて学校をやめてしまった。
あそびたくて学校を休んでしまった。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 4 is correct. It means "I skipped school because I wanted to play (goof off)."

**Question Translation**  
Last week, I skipped school.

**Explanation**  
- さぼる（サボる） means "to slack off; to skip (class/work); to play truant."  
- 学校をサボる specifically means intentionally not attending school for non-legitimate reasons (e.g., to have fun).  
- Answer 4 あそびたくて学校を休んでしまった conveys the same idea: taking a day off from school because you wanted to play, matching the intentional/truant nuance of サボる. The 〜てしまった adds a sense of completion/regret, which fits the tone.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: 病気になって学校をやめてしまった。  
  Means "I got sick and ended up quitting school." やめる = quit entirely, not skip. Also sickness is not implied by サボる.
- Answer 2: 病気になって学校を休んでしまった。  
  Means "I became sick and ended up taking a day off school." Absence due to illness is legitimate, not "サボる" (intentional truancy).
- Answer 3: あそびたくて学校をやめてしまった。  
  Means "I wanted to play so I quit school." やめる = quit completely; サボる is temporary skipping.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
- サボる（さぼる）: colloquial, "to skip/slack off" (often from school/work).  
- 学校を休む = be absent/take a day off; 学校をやめる = quit/withdraw from school.  
- 〜たくて: "because I want to ..." expresses reason.  
- 〜てしまう: completion or regret/unintended outcome nuance.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「あそびたくて学校を休んでしまった」 — 놀고 싶어서 학교를 (하루) 쉬어 버렸다.
근거: 「サボる」=땡땡이치다(정당한 이유 없이 의도적으로 빠짐). 그만둔 게 아니라 그날 빠진 것.
오답: 1·2 病気(병)는 정당한 결석 이유이므로 「サボる」가 아님. 3 やめる(그만두다)는 학교를 영원히 그만두는 의미로 어긋남.
